context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Security and Post-Conflict Development and Review

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the nature of the relationship between development interventions before, during and after conflict, and the factors that drive conflict itself? Does the rise of new global powers like China portend a transformation of the development-security agenda as conventionally practiced? Does it make sense to push for more and more convergence of security and development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m trying to figure out this 700–800 word reflection on security and post-conflict development. I’m a little stuck on the main argument, especially how to connect development interventions to conflict drivers and then bring China into it.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you've identified the three moving parts of the prompt well. Let's start with the foundation. Before thinking about what development interventions *do*, what do you think *drives* conflict in the first place? What comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning and needs to organize their thinking. Rather than scaffolding all three parts of the prompt at once, I should focus them on the first and most foundational question: the relationship between development interventions and conflict drivers. Using the Socratic method, I'll ask them a focused opening question to get them thinking independently before we build toward China and the convergence debate.

exchanges.student: I think stuff like poverty, inequality, weak government, corruption, and maybe exclusion of certain ethnic or political groups? I’m not totally sure if I should treat those as “root causes” versus just conditions that make conflict more likely.
exchanges.tutor: That's a useful distinction to hold onto. Now, if poverty, inequality, and weak governance are among the things that drive conflict — what does that suggest about what development interventions are actually *targeting* when they operate in conflict-affected settings?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely good observation about the distinction between root causes and enabling conditions. This is a meaningful conceptual tension worth briefly acknowledging, but I shouldn't dwell on it too long. I want to push them forward toward the key question: if those are the drivers, what does that imply about the relationship between development interventions and conflict? This moves us toward the core of the prompt.

exchanges.student: I guess they’re supposed to target the conditions that can feed conflict, not just “development” in a neutral way. So maybe things like service delivery, jobs, institution-building, and inclusion are meant to reduce grievances or make the state seem more legitimate.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've got the logic running in one direction. But can you think of ways development interventions might actually *fuel* or *worsen* conflict, rather than reduce it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress connecting development interventions to conflict drivers through the lens of legitimacy and grievance reduction. This is solid thinking that directly addresses the first part of the prompt. Now I should push them to think about the more complex and critical angle: can development interventions also *trigger* or *worsen* conflict, not just reduce it? This complicates their argument and makes for a richer reflection.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think so. If aid or projects get distributed unevenly, that could deepen resentment, and if outside actors back one government or faction, that might make the conflict worse. I’m also thinking development projects can be tied to security goals so much that local people stop seeing them as neutral or legitimate.
exchanges.tutor: You've got a strong foundation there — development interventions cut both ways. Now, how does China fit in? What do you know about how China's approach to development in conflict-affected or fragile states differs from the more conventional Western model?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now built a solid two-sided picture of the development-conflict relationship: interventions can reduce conflict drivers but also exacerbate them. This is the core of the first prompt question. Rather than dwelling further on this, I should acknowledge their progress and pivot them toward the China question, which is the second major component of the prompt and one they said they were stuck on.

exchanges.student: I mostly think of China as emphasizing infrastructure, investment, and non-interference instead of governance reform or political conditionality. I’m just not sure if that means it really changes the development-security agenda, or if it just gives states another donor with different rules.
exchanges.tutor: That's the right tension to sit with. Think about it this way: if the conventional Western model links development aid to governance reform and security goals, what does it mean for that agenda when recipient states have the option to get development financing *without* those conditions attached?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified China's key characteristics (infrastructure, non-interference, no conditionality) and is now wrestling with the 'so what' question — does China actually transform the development-security agenda or just add another option? This is exactly the right tension to push on. I should help them think through the implications of China's model for the conventional security-development nexus, without giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: I think it weakens the monopoly of the Western model, since states can avoid some governance or security conditions by turning to China. But I’m unsure whether that is automatically good or bad, because it could increase state autonomy while also reducing pressure for accountability.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — and that tension is worth keeping in your reflection. Now, given everything you've thought through, what's your instinct on the third question: does pushing for more convergence between security and development actually make sense?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is thinking critically and has identified a genuine trade-off. This is good analytical thinking that serves the reflection well. We've now covered the first two parts of the prompt substantively. I should pivot to the third question — whether it makes sense to push for more convergence of security and development — which is where their argument needs a conclusion or stance. I'll ask them to start forming a position.

exchanges.student: My instinct is “only up to a point.” It seems useful when insecurity clearly blocks livelihoods and institutions, but too much convergence can militarize development and make aid serve short-term stability instead of justice or long-term political change.
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely strong position — conditional convergence with a clear-eyed view of the risks. You now have substantive thinking on all three parts of the prompt. I'd encourage you to try writing a draft. What's your opening move — how do you want to frame the reflection from the start?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student now has a clear, nuanced position on all three parts of the prompt. They have the raw material for a strong reflection. We've had about 6 substantive exchanges and covered all three components. Rather than continuing to explore ideas, I should now push them to integrate everything into a written attempt, since we are approaching the point where continued discussion without writing risks dwelling on ideas rather than producing the actual assignment.

exchanges.student: I think I want to open by saying conflict and development shape each other, instead of treating conflict as just a breakdown that development fixes afterward. I’m not sure how to phrase that in a cleaner thesis though.
exchanges.tutor: That framing is strong — the bidirectional relationship is exactly the right angle. But the phrasing has to come from you. Try writing a sentence or two and share it here — even a rough attempt is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid conceptual framing for their opening but is asking me to help them phrase it — which would cross into writing for them. I should redirect them to do the writing themselves while affirming the strength of the idea and perhaps nudging them to think about what specific word or concept captures the bidirectional relationship they're describing.

exchanges.student: Maybe: “The relationship between development and conflict is not linear, because development interventions can both reduce and intensify the conditions that produce violence. Before, during, and after conflict, development is tied to questions of state legitimacy, inequality, and external power, which means the development-security nexus should be treated cautiously rather than assumed to be beneficial.”
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid thesis — it captures the bidirectionality and signals your cautious stance on convergence. One thing to consider: reading just this opening, would a reader know that China and the shift in global power are part of your argument? Think about whether your thesis needs to gesture toward that dimension as well.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid opening thesis that captures the bidirectional relationship and gestures toward the development-security nexus. It's genuinely good work. Rather than over-praising or picking it apart excessively, I should give honest feedback on its strengths and one area to think about — specifically whether China and the transformation of the agenda is flagged enough in the opening, since that's a major part of the prompt. I should not rewrite anything for them.

exchanges.student: Oh right, I left that out. Maybe I should add that China exposes the limits of the conventional Western model by offering development without the same political conditions, which complicates the push for convergence.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — that addition ties all three parts of the prompt together in your opening. You now have everything you need to write a full draft. Go write it, and come back if you hit a specific roadblock.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now identified what's missing and has the right idea for how to integrate it. This is good self-correction. Since we're at exchange 9 and the student has all the ingredients for a strong draft, I should encourage them to now go write and not keep refining the thesis verbally. The conversation has been productive and it's time to push them to produce the actual text.
